Skip to contents
bayesma 0.0.0.9000
  • Get Started
  • Reference
    • Models overview
    • Common-effect & random-effects
    • Binary outcomes: one-stage vs two-stage
    • Bias-corrected model (Jung/Verde)
    • Selection models (Copas; weight functions)
    • PET–PEESE
    • Robust mixtures
    • RoBMA (stub)
  • Changelog

Discussed and sketched a comprehensive refactoring of Stan code generation in the bayesma R package, consolidating 16 generator functions into 7 using a block-builder pattern and consolidated robma spec_component functions. Reviewed the PET-PEESE implementation’s use of sample-size-based predictors (1/sqrt(n) and 1/n), confirming it aligns with both Stanley (2017) and Pustejovsky (2017) simulation evidence showing these are preferable to SE-based predictors for avoiding mechanical correlation between d and V.

Source: conversation-export.md

User

Within the R/ folder are functions for a Bayesian Meta Analysis using Stan. Every new function seems to be bigger than the ones previous. One of the issues is the construction of the Stan model. There are numerous different functions for each type of model that is being constructed. I’m not sure if this is normal or not, but certain parts of the Stan code don’t change (eg data). I was wondering if it were feasible or a good idea to have a generate_stan_model function that takes different arguments (eg model_type, stage, etc) that feed into the model or whether it is better (and easier to debug) with the model construction the way it is.

What do you think?

Databot

The user wants my opinion on refactoring their Stan model construction code. Let me first explore the codebase to understand the current structure before giving advice.

Good question. Let me look at the current structure before giving you an informed opinion.

Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me give a thoughtful opinion on whether to refactor into a unified generate_stan_model() function.

This is a really well-informed question, and having looked through your code, I can give you a grounded answer.

Short answer: a unified generate_stan_model() is feasible and probably worth doing, but with caveats.

What you’d gain

Your current setup has ~15 generator functions that all follow the same block structure (data → parameters → transformed parameters → model → generated quantities) and share significant overlap. A few concrete examples of duplication:

  • The skew_normal_lpdf_custom function block is copy-pasted identically between generate_stan_code_two_stage() and generate_stan_code_one_stage()
  • The two-stage data block (int S; vector y; vector se;) appears in at least 5 places with minor conditional additions
  • Generated quantities blocks (pooled, mu_new, y_rep) are largely standardized

A composable approach would reduce this duplication and make it easier to add new model features without creating yet another function.

What I’d actually recommend

Rather than one monolithic generate_stan_model() with many arguments, I’d suggest a block-builder pattern — you’re already partway there with helpers like emit_prior_target() and emit_robust_likelihood(). The idea would be:

# Each block has its own builder
build_data_block(stage, likelihood, use_t_likelihood, use_multi_arm, ...)
build_parameters_block(model_type, re_dist, use_robust, ...)
build_transformed_parameters_block(...)
build_model_block(...)
build_generated_quantities_block(...)

# Then a thin orchestrator
generate_stan_model <- function(stage, model_type, likelihood, re_dist, ...) {
  parts <- c(
    build_functions_block(re_dist),
    build_data_block(stage, likelihood, ...),
    build_parameters_block(model_type, re_dist, ...),
    build_transformed_parameters_block(stage, model_type, ...),
    build_model_block(stage, likelihood, model_type, ...),
    build_generated_quantities_block(stage, model_type, ...)
  )
  paste(parts[nzchar(parts)], collapse = "\n\n")
}

This keeps each block independently testable and debuggable while eliminating the duplication.

The honest tradeoff

The risk is that a unified generator with many conditionals can become harder to reason about than separate functions — “what Stan code does this combination of arguments produce?” becomes a non-trivial question. Your current approach, while repetitive, has the virtue that each function is self-contained and readable top-to-bottom.

A few practical guidelines if you go ahead:

  1. Write snapshot tests — for each model configuration you support, store the expected Stan code as a text file and test against it. This is the single most important thing for debuggability. testthat::expect_snapshot() works well for this.
  2. Keep the robma spec_component_* functions separate for now. They have enough unique structure (returning list(stan_code, stan_data)) that forcing them into the same generator would add complexity without much benefit.
  3. Start with the two-stage and one-stage generators, which share the most overlap. The meta-regression and Egger functions can follow later.
  4. Use the vector-collection pattern (c() then paste(collapse)) consistently rather than mixing it with paste0 concatenation and glue. Your meta-regression functions already use this pattern well.

So: yes, it’s feasible and a good idea, but I’d approach it incrementally — consolidate the most duplicated pieces first, keep good test coverage, and don’t try to unify everything in one pass.

Want me to sketch out what the block builders might look like concretely for your two-stage and one-stage generators?

User

Yes please

Databot

The user wants me to sketch out concrete block builders for their two-stage and one-stage Stan generators. Let me read the actual code for these functions to understand the exact conditionals and patterns.

Let me read the actual generator functions to build something concrete against your real code.

Now let me also quickly check the existing helper functions for emitting priors and robust code.

Now let me check the existing emit helpers so I know what’s available to build on.

Good, I’ve got a thorough picture. Let me now write the sketch.

I’ve written the sketch to R/generate_stan_model_sketch.R. Here’s the rationale behind the design decisions:

Structure

The core idea is a context object (ctx) — a list that holds all the configuration (stage, model_type, re_dist, likelihood, priors, flags). Every builder receives this single object rather than threading 8+ arguments through every call. This makes it easy to add new flags later without changing every function signature.

The hierarchy is:

  • generate_stan_model() — thin orchestrator, calls block builders, assembles
  • build_*_block() — one per Stan block (data, parameters, model, etc.)
  • Sub-helpers like build_re_parameters(), build_likelihood_two_stage() — factored out where a block has substantial internal branching

What’s shared vs. separate

Things that are now shared (were duplicated before): - The skew_normal custom function - Data block core structure (with stage-specific additions) - RE parameter declarations (tau/z/nu/alpha_skew pattern) - RE prior emission logic - Generated quantities patterns (skewness stats, mu_new, y_rep)

Things I kept separate deliberately: - The mixture likelihood code for each stage (structurally distinct enough that abstracting further would obscure more than it clarifies) - The multi-arm transformed parameters block (complex loop logic that’s better as a readable unit) - The robust one-stage likelihood per distribution family (each has different approximation logic)

What this doesn’t cover

The sketch handles generate_stan_code_two_stage, generate_stan_code_two_stage_null, and generate_stan_code_one_stage. It does not attempt to fold in the meta-regression generators or the robma spec_component_* functions — those would be a separate phase if you decide this pattern works.

Next steps if you want to proceed

  1. Write snapshot tests for every model configuration you currently support, using the existing generators to produce the “expected” output
  2. Implement the new builders and verify they produce identical Stan code
  3. Swap call sites one at a time

Want me to start on any of those, or would you rather iterate on the sketch first?

User

So out of the 15 functions, how many will this sketch be able to handle?

Databot

The user wants to know how many of the 15 generator functions the sketch covers. Let me map it out based on what I learned from the exploration.

From the exploration, the 15 functions were:

  1. generate_stan_code_two_stage() — ✅ covered

  2. generate_stan_code_two_stage_null() — ✅ covered (via null_model = TRUE)

  3. generate_stan_code_one_stage() — ✅ covered

  4. generate_stan_code_bias_corrected() — ❌ not covered

  5. generate_stan_code_mreg_two_stage() — ❌ not covered

  6. generate_stan_code_mreg_one_stage() — ❌ not covered

  7. generate_bayesian_egger_stan_binomial() — ❌ not covered

  8. generate_bayesian_egger_stan_generic() — ❌ not covered 9-17. spec_component_* functions (robma) — ❌ not covered

So the sketch covers 3 out of the 15 functions, with the remaining 12 being either specialized variants for mediation regression, Egger regression, or robustness analysis components.

3 out of 15 — specifically:

# Function Covered? How
1 generate_stan_code_two_stage() Yes stage = "two_stage"
2 generate_stan_code_two_stage_null() Yes stage = "two_stage", null_model = TRUE
3 generate_stan_code_one_stage() Yes stage = "one_stage"
4 generate_stan_code_bias_corrected() No
5 generate_stan_code_mreg_two_stage() No
6 generate_stan_code_mreg_one_stage() No
7 generate_bayesian_egger_stan_binomial() No
8 generate_bayesian_egger_stan_generic() No
9–17 spec_component_* (9 robma functions) No

The meta-regression generators (5–6) would be the most natural next addition since they share a lot of structure with the core two-stage/one-stage generators — mainly adding a design matrix X and coefficient vector beta. The Egger and robma spec functions are more structurally distinct and would take more work to unify.

That said, even consolidating just those 3 functions is meaningful — they’re the largest and most complex of the group, and the ones most likely to grow as you add features.

User

I’m not sure why the meta regression model can’t be used by this sketch? It’s only the addition of a list of covariates, isn’t it?

Databot

You’re right — let me check exactly what differs.

You’re basically right. The meta-regression generators are simpler than the base ones (normal RE only, no robust, no multi-arm, no mixture) — they just add beta, X, and per-coefficient priors. The differences are:

  1. Data block: adds int K and matrix[S, K] X (or matrix[N, K] X for one-stage)
  2. Parameters block: adds vector[K] beta
  3. Transformed parameters: theta = mu + X * beta + tau * z instead of mu + tau * z
  4. Model block: per-coefficient beta priors (with the nice detail of collapsing to a single vectorised prior when all betas share the same prior)
  5. Likelihood (one-stage): eta includes X[i] * beta term

These are all small, additive changes. The sketch could handle this with a use_meta_reg flag (or passing a moderators config) that conditionally injects K, X, beta, and the beta priors. Let me update it.

Now let me update each block builder to handle the meta-regression additions.

Now update the model block to handle beta priors:

Now add the build_beta_priors helper and update the likelihood to include the X * beta term for one-stage meta-regression:

Now update the one-stage likelihood eta term and one-stage transformed parameters to include X[i] * beta:

Now do the same for the y_rep eta in generated quantities:

Finally, for the two-stage meta-regression, the likelihood uses theta which already incorporates X * beta via transformed parameters, so that path needs no change. But the two-stage y_rep should use theta[i] when meta-regression is enabled:

There’s also a minor detail — the two-stage meta-regression uses student_t_rng for y_rep when t-likelihood is on (your current code does this). Let me also handle the two-stage meta-reg likelihood, which goes through theta and is already covered by build_likelihood_two_stage. The only nuance is that the current mreg two-stage code uses sampling syntax (y ~ normal(theta, se)) rather than target +=, but that’s semantically equivalent — we can standardise on target +=.

So with these changes, the sketch now covers 5 out of 15:

# Function How
1 generate_stan_code_two_stage() stage = "two_stage"
2 generate_stan_code_two_stage_null() stage = "two_stage", null_model = TRUE
3 generate_stan_code_one_stage() stage = "one_stage"
4 generate_stan_code_mreg_two_stage() stage = "two_stage", mreg_config = list(enabled = TRUE)
5 generate_stan_code_mreg_one_stage() stage = "one_stage", mreg_config = list(enabled = TRUE)

You were right that it was a straightforward addition. The meta-regression generators are essentially the base generators with beta, X, and the X * beta term injected in a few predictable places. The only slightly tricky bit is the per-coefficient beta prior logic, which I factored into build_beta_priors().

The updated sketch is in R/generate_stan_model_sketch.R. Want to look at bringing the Egger or bias-corrected generators into the fold next, or focus on getting tests in place for these 5?

User

Yeah, try those 2.

Databot

Let me read the Egger and bias-corrected generator functions to understand their structure.

Now I have a clear picture. Let me assess these two:

  1. generate_stan_code_bias_corrected() — This is a very specialized model (bias correction with biased/unbiased mixture, inverse variance parameterization, known/unknown bias indicators). It has unique parameters (B, p_bias, w_bias_raw, theta_raw_unbiased, theta_raw_biased), unique transformed parameters (biased/unbiased theta), and a unique likelihood structure (mixture of biased/unbiased components). This is structurally very different from the core meta-analysis models.

  2. Egger functions — These are also fundamentally distinct, with their own parameter sets and likelihood structures. The binomial version computes transformed event rates from latent parameters, while the generic version uses a simpler regression approach. Both support multiplicative and additive heterogeneity variants, and they implement their own prior handling rather than relying on the standard emit_prior_target function.

These are both completely different model architectures — not variations on the core pooling approach. Forcing them into a single generate_stan_model() function would require so many conditional branches that it would actually obscure the logic rather than clarify it.

Having read both in detail, I’d actually push back on folding these into the unified generator. Here’s why:

generate_stan_code_bias_corrected() is a fundamentally different model structure. It has biased/unbiased mixture components with separate theta vectors, inverse-variance parameterisation for tau, per-study bias weights (w_bias_raw), and a known-vs-unknown-bias branching likelihood. Almost nothing is shared with the core generators — different data, different parameters, different transformed parameters, different likelihood, different generated quantities.

The Egger functions are similarly distinct. The binomial version has latent event rates (p0, p1), computes sigma from the latent rates in transformed parameters, fits a hierarchical model for study-specific log ORs (delta ~ normal(d, tau)), and then runs an Egger regression on top. The generic version is a simple weighted regression. Both use their own local prior_to_stan() helper rather than emit_prior_target(). And both branch on multiplicative vs additive heterogeneity.

The core generators that the sketch unifies all share the same fundamental structure: pool study effects with optional random effects, optionally adjust for moderators. The Egger and bias-corrected models are different statistical models entirely — not variations on the same skeleton.

If you forced them into generate_stan_model(), you’d end up with something like:

if (ctx$model_family == "egger" && ctx$egger_type == "binomial" && 
    ctx$heterogeneity == "multiplicative") {
  # completely different data block
  # completely different parameters
  # completely different everything
}

At that point the “unified” function is just a dispatch table, and you’ve traded readable self-contained functions for a large function where unrelated models are interleaved through conditionals.

What I’d recommend instead: leave these as standalone generators, but extract the small bits they do share:

  1. Replace the local prior_to_stan() in the Egger functions with emit_prior_target() (they do the same thing)
  2. The Egger generic model is simple enough that multiplicative vs additive could be one function with a single conditional on the heterogeneity variance term, rather than two large glue blocks that differ by one line

Those are targeted cleanups that reduce duplication without forcing unlike models into the same framework.

Refactor the Egger functions to use emit_prior_target() and consolidate the multiplicative/additive variants.

Write snapshot tests for the 5 generators the sketch covers before swapping in the new code.

Look at the robma spec_component_* functions to see if any of those share enough structure to consolidate.

User

What about the selection models and pet_peese?

Databot

Now I have a complete picture of all the robma spec_component functions. Let me assess which ones could be unified.

Looking at the patterns:

Selection weight models (spec_component_selection_weight, spec_component_selection_weight_fe, spec_component_selection_weight_h0): - These three are very similar. The RE vs FE difference is just whether tau exists and whether sigma_i = sqrt(tau^2 + se^2) or just se[i]. The H0 variant fixes mu=0. The weight function likelihood logic (the p-value binning, omega weights, normalisation constant) is essentially identical across all three. These could absolutely be unified into one function with has_re and null_model flags.

  • The PET-PEESE models are also candidates for consolidation—they differ mainly in whether mu is included and whether the bias correction uses inverse square root of sample size or inverse sample size.
  • The Copas and bias-corrected models follow similar patterns where random effects versus fixed effects comes down to whether tau is estimated and how sigma is computed.
  • All of these are spec_component functions that feed into the robma model averaging framework, which is structurally distinct from the core meta-analysis generators.
  • I’m wondering if they could be unified into their own family with a dedicated generate_robma_component() dispatcher rather than folding them into the main generator.
  • Looking at the actual structure across PET-PEESE and selection weight models, there’s a consistent pattern: they all take N, y, se as core data, add optional mu and tau parameters, then apply a bias term to the normal likelihood with either se[i] or sigma_i as the scale.
  • The selection weight models layer a weight function on top of this foundation, so the question becomes whether this shared structure could be abstracted into a more unified approach.

Actually, these models are fundamentally different enough that they shouldn’t merge into the main generator—the selection weight models have a completely different likelihood structure with the weight function and normalisation constant, and the Copas model introduces yet another selection function entirely. But within each family, there’s clear duplication I could collapse: the PET-PEESE variants could become one function parameterized by null_model and bias_covariate, the selection weight variants could be unified with has_re and null_model flags, and the Copas models could follow a similar pattern.

Looking at the consolidation potential, I could reduce the 9 robma spec_component functions down to 4 by collapsing these redundancies within each family—that’s a meaningful simplification worth presenting clearly.

These are interesting — they’re structurally different from the core generators, but they share a lot with each other. The robma components all follow a similar skeleton and vary along two axes: has_re (RE vs FE) and null_model (mu estimated vs mu=0).

Here’s what I see after reading them all:

PET-PEESE family (3 functions → 1)

spec_component_pet_peese, spec_component_pet_peese_h0, and spec_component_peese_h0 are nearly identical. The differences are:

mu bias covariate has_re branching
pet_peese (H1) estimated 1/sqrt(n) yes
pet_peese_h0 0 1/sqrt(n) yes
peese_h0 0 1/n yes

These could trivially collapse into one function with null_model and bias_transform = c("inv_sqrt_n", "inv_n") arguments.

Selection weight family (3 functions → 1)

spec_component_selection_weight, spec_component_selection_weight_fe, and spec_component_selection_weight_h0 share the same weight-function likelihood logic almost verbatim — the p-value binning, omega weights, and normalisation constant are copy-pasted across all three. The only differences:

  • Whether tau exists (RE vs FE)
  • Whether mu is estimated or fixed at 0
  • The variance term in the likelihood: sigma_i = sqrt(tau^2 + se^2) vs just se[i]
  • The normalisation constant computation adjusts slightly for tau

One function with has_re + null_model would handle all three.

Copas and bias-corrected

spec_component_selection_copas already handles RE/FE via has_re — no change needed. spec_component_bias_corrected + spec_component_jung_h0 could merge into one with a null_model flag, though the RE bias-corrected model (which delegates to generate_stan_code_bias_corrected()) is substantially more complex than the others.

Summary

These wouldn’t go into generate_stan_model() — they’re different statistical models, not variations on “pool with optional RE.” But they could consolidate among themselves:

Current Proposed Reduction
pet_peese + pet_peese_h0 + peese_h0 spec_component_pet_peese() 3 → 1
selection_weight + selection_weight_fe + selection_weight_h0 spec_component_selection_weight() 3 → 1
selection_copas unchanged 1 → 1
bias_corrected + jung_h0 spec_component_bias_corrected() 2 → 1

That takes the 9 robma functions from 9 → 4. Combined with the earlier 5 → 1 consolidation of the core generators, the total generator count goes from 15 → 5 (plus the Egger functions, which I’d leave as-is with minor cleanup).

Want me to sketch out the consolidated PET-PEESE and selection weight functions? Those two are the most clear-cut wins.

User

Do all of those. Thanks

Databot

Let me re-read the current sketch file to see where to append, and double-check one detail on the jung_h0 function.

Now let me write all three consolidated functions and append them to the sketch file.

Now let me read the end of the file to know where to append:

The selection weight normalisation constant is the trickiest part — it differs subtly across the three original functions depending on whether tau is present and whether mu is 0. Let me look more carefully at the exact expressions.

Now I can see the four variants of the normalisation constant clearly:

  1. H1 + RE (selection_weight): Phi((z_bounds[k] * se[i] - mu) / sigma[i]) - Phi((z_bounds[k+1] * se[i] - mu) / sigma[i])

  2. H1 + FE (selection_weight_fe): Phi((z_bounds[k] * se[i] - mu) / se[i]) - Phi((z_bounds[k+1] * se[i] - mu) / se[i]) which simplifies to Phi(z_bounds[k] - mu/se[i]) - Phi(z_bounds[k+1] - mu/se[i])

  3. H0 + RE (selection_weight_h0 with random effects): Phi(z_bounds[k] * se[i] / sigma_i) - Phi(z_bounds[k+1] * se[i] / sigma_i) using the local sigma_i rather than the vector

  4. H0 + FE (selection_weight_h0 without random effects): Phi(z_bounds[k]) - Phi(z_bounds[k+1]) — the simplest form with no mean or variance adjustments

The pattern shows how the normalization constant changes depending on whether we’re testing under the null or alternative hypothesis and whether random effects are included. I’m noticing a key difference in how sigma is handled between the two variants—the H0+RE model computes it locally in the loop as a derived quantity, while H1+RE defines it in the transformed parameters block, which affects how the general expression gets instantiated in each case.

For the consolidated version, I’ll standardize on using transformed parameters for sigma when random effects are present, so the Phi argument can follow a consistent pattern based on whether we have random effects and whether it’s a null model.

Building the string expressions for the Phi arguments…

Now I’m realizing the mathematical equivalence—when there’s no random effect and no null model, the generated expression simplifies to just the z-bounds directly, which matches what the current code does. Rather than creating separate helper functions, I’ll build the normalization constant lines inline with string interpolation for clarity. I also need to handle the likelihood computation, particularly how sigma gets calculated in the random effects case.

Good — now I can see the exact normalisation constant expressions across the 4 variants. The general form is Phi((z_bounds[k] * se[i] - mu) / scale) where scale is sigma[i] (RE) or se[i] (FE), and mu is 0 for null models. Let me fix the selection weight function — I used placeholder helper functions that don’t exist. I’ll also add the bias-corrected consolidation.

Now let me find the start and end of the selection weight function to replace it entirely:

Now I’ll replace the selection weight function with the correct normalisation constant expression, and append the bias-corrected consolidation:

All three are in R/generate_stan_model_sketch.R. Here’s a summary of how they map to the originals:

spec_component_pet_peese() — replaces 3 functions

Original call Consolidated call
spec_component_pet_peese(has_re=T/F) spec_component_pet_peese(has_re=T/F)
spec_component_pet_peese_h0(has_re=T/F) spec_component_pet_peese(has_re=T/F, null_model=TRUE)
spec_component_peese_h0(has_re=T/F) spec_component_pet_peese(has_re=T/F, null_model=TRUE, bias_transform="inv_n")

The only structural difference between these three was: (1) whether mu is estimated or 0, and (2) whether the bias covariate is 1/sqrt(n) or 1/n. Both are now arguments.

spec_component_selection_weight() — replaces 3 functions

Original call Consolidated call
spec_component_selection_weight() spec_component_selection_weight(has_re=TRUE)
spec_component_selection_weight_fe() spec_component_selection_weight(has_re=FALSE)
spec_component_selection_weight_h0(has_re=T/F) spec_component_selection_weight(has_re=T/F, null_model=TRUE)

The weight-function likelihood logic (p-value binning, omega assignment, normalisation constant) was copy-pasted across all three. Now it’s written once. The normalisation constant uses the general form Phi((z_bounds[k] * se[i] - mu) / scale) which is mathematically equivalent to the simplified forms in the originals.

spec_component_bias_corrected() — replaces 2 functions

Original call Consolidated call
spec_component_bias_corrected(has_re=T/F) spec_component_bias_corrected(has_re=T/F)
spec_component_jung_h0(has_re=T/F) spec_component_bias_corrected(has_re=T/F, null_model=TRUE)

The RE + H1 case still delegates to generate_stan_code_bias_corrected() because it’s substantially more complex (inverse-variance parameterisation, per-study bias weights). The other three variants (FE+H1, RE+H0, FE+H0) are unified.

Overall tally

With spec_component_selection_copas unchanged (it already handles RE/FE via has_re), plus the Egger functions left as-is, the full picture is now:

Group Before After
Core generators 5 1 (generate_stan_model)
Robma components 9 4
Egger 2 2 (cleanup only)
Total 16 7

Write snapshot tests capturing the Stan code output of each original function before swapping in the consolidated versions.

Refactor the Egger functions to use emit_prior_target() and consolidate the multiplicative/additive variants.

Review the call sites in robma_bayesma.R to plan how to update them to use the new consolidated function signatures.

User

Are the PET-PEESE models using SE & SE^2 or are they using 1/sqrt(n) & 1/n?

Databot

They’re using 1/sqrt(n) and 1/n. Looking at the code:

  • spec_component_pet_peese computes inv_sqrt_n[i] = 1.0 / sqrt(n_total[i]) in transformed data, then regresses on that
  • spec_component_peese_h0 computes inv_n[i] = 1.0 / n_total[i]

This is a sample-size-based approach rather than the traditional PET-PEESE formulation which uses SE and SE^2 as the bias covariates. The two approaches are related but not identical — 1/sqrt(n) is proportional to SE only under specific assumptions (e.g., equal variances across groups, equal group sizes).

Was that intentional, or would you prefer the standard SE-based version? The SE-based version would be simpler since se is already in the data block — no need for n_total or a transformed data block at all.

User

It was changed as per Stanley (2017) as a variance stabilised predictor. Is this wrong?

Databot

It’s not wrong — it’s a defensible choice. Stanley & Doucouliagos (2014, 2017) do argue that 1/sqrt(n) is a variance-stabilised predictor for the PET test, and 1/n for PEESE, on the grounds that SE itself is a noisy estimate (especially with small samples) and using it as both the weight and the predictor creates a mechanical correlation. Using sample size avoids that endogeneity problem.

That said, the literature isn’t fully settled on this. The original PET-PEESE formulation (Stanley & Doucouliagos, 2012) used SE and SE^2, and many implementations still do (e.g., metafor::regtest, the RoBMA package). The sample-size-based version assumes a fairly direct relationship between 1/sqrt(n) and SE, which holds cleanly for standardised mean differences but is more approximate for log odds ratios or log risk ratios where SE depends on event rates as well as sample size.

Since your package supports binomial and Poisson likelihoods alongside Gaussian, it might be worth noting this assumption in the documentation — that the 1/sqrt(n) predictor is most directly motivated for continuous outcomes. But for the robma context where these are component models being averaged, the approximation is likely fine.

Short answer: you’re on solid ground citing Stanley (2017). Just be transparent about the choice.

User

What about something like this: https://jepusto.com/posts/PET-PEESE-performance/

Databot

This is Pustejovsky’s 2017 blog post comparing traditional PET-PEESE (using SE and SE²) vs sample-size variants (SPET/SPEESE using 1/√n and 1/n). The key findings support what bayesma is doing:

  1. Traditional PET/PEESE using SE/SE² have wildly incorrect Type-I error rates because d and V are mechanically correlated

  2. Sample-size variants (SPET/SPEESE using 1/√n and 1/n) maintain correct Type-I error rates

  3. SPET/SPEESE are exactly unbiased in the absence of publication bias

  4. Under publication bias, SPEESE emerges as the recommended default even though no single estimator consistently outperforms the others

So Pustejovsky’s analysis directly validates the sample-size approach that bayesma is using.

This actually reinforces your choice. Pustejovsky’s simulations show exactly why you’d want the sample-size variants:

  1. Traditional PET/PEESE (using SE and SE²) have badly inflated Type-I error rates even with no publication bias, because d and V are mechanically correlated — you need d to compute V
  2. SPET/SPEESE (using 1/sqrt(n) and 1/n) maintain correct Type-I error rates and are exactly unbiased when there’s no publication bias
  3. Under actual publication bias, neither variant consistently dominates, but Pustejovsky’s “gun to my head” recommendation is SPEESE (1/n)

So your implementation aligns with both Stanley (2017) and Pustejovsky’s independent simulation evidence. The one thing worth noting from the post is his observation that the 1/sqrt(n) predictor is specifically motivated for standardized mean differences from two-group designs — which circles back to the caveat I mentioned about log ORs and log RRs, where the SE-to-sample-size relationship is more complex. But within the robma model-averaging framework, where these are component models weighted by evidence, this is a reasonable approximation.

If anything, you might consider documenting both the Stanley (2017) and Pustejovsky (2017) references in your PET-PEESE function documentation to explain the choice.

User

I thhink RoBMA does a z-transformation first to avaoid the problem. Here is the package site:

https://fbartos.github.io/RoBMA/

Databot

Your Posit AI credits have been depleted.

On this page
